coredata.dc:title: Highly stable Si MOSFET-type humidity sensor with ink-jet printed graphene quantum dots sensing layer
coredata.dc:creator: Jeong, Yujeong
Hong, Seongbin
Jung, Gyuweon
Shin, Wonjun
Park, Jinwoo
Kim, Donghee
Choi, Yong Seok
Bae, Jong-Ho
Hong, Byung Hee
Lee, Jong-Ho
coredata.dc:description: This paper investigates humidity sensing characteristics of a silicon metal oxide semiconductor field effect transistor (Si MOSFET)-based humidity sensor having horizontal floating-gate (FG) interdigitated with control-gate (CG). The sensing material of the humidity sensor is graphene quantum dots (GQDs), deposited locally on the interdigitated CG-FG area by an ink-jet printing method using a small amount of the GQDs solution. The humidity sensing characteristics of the sensor are measured as a parameter of relative humidity (RH). The response of the humidity sensor is 78 % to the humid air of 81.3 % RH. We also adopt a pulsed pre-bias method to improve the response and recovery characteristics of the humidity sensor. The response and recovery characteristics of the sensor can be improved 30 % and 40 % respectively by applying a pre-bias of 2 V and -1 V to the CG. In all relative humidity ranges, the FET-type humidity sensor has highly stable and reproducible characteristics in long-term measurements for 5 months.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FET-type gas sensor
Humidity sensor
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Pulsed pre-bias

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Humidity sensing performance of the FET-type sensors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Brain Korea 21 Plus Project in 2021 and the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF-2016R1A2B3009361 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic 3D birds’ eye view of the fabricated FET-type humidity sensor with ink-jet printed GQDs sensing layer. (b) Microscopic image of the FET-type humidity sensor with ink-jet printed GQDs sensing layer. (c) Cross-sectional view of the humidity sensor cut along a dashed line A-A’ in (b).
(a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image, (b) size distribution, (c) X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), and (d) Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy(FT-IR) of the synthesized GQDs.

(a) Transfer characteristics (I D-V CG) of the FET-type humidity sensor as a parameter of the relative humidity (from 17.5 % RH to 81.3 % RH) at 20 °C. (b) Schematic illustration of the humidity sensing mechanism of the humidity sensor with the GQDs sensing layer. At high relative humidity, sensing mechanism is based on the physisorption of the water molecules.

(a) Transient I D behaviors and (b) response of the FET-type humidity sensor as a parameter of the relative humidity (17.5-81.3 % RH) at 20 °C. As the relative humidity increases, the response of humidity increases, but its slope gradually decreases. (c) |I D| curve of the humidity sensor as a function of the relative humidity from 3.4 % RH to 81.3 % RH. Inset shows the linear fitting of the |I D| curve in the lower humidity range (3.4-47.2 % RH).

(a) Transient I D behaviors of the humidity sensor as a parameter of the operating temperature in the humid air of 81.3 % RH. (b) Response of the humidity sensor as a parameter of the operating temperature to the humid air with relative humidity ranging from 28.3 % RH to 81.3 % RH. (c) Response and response time of the humidity sensor as a function of operating temperature in the humid air of 81.3 % RH.

(a) Schematic illustration of the pulse generator applied to the CG and drain of the FET-type humidity sensor, and pulse scheme for the pre-bias measurement. The V pre is applied to the CG during t pre. The V rDS with a pulse width of tread is synchronized with the V rCG. (b) Transient I D behaviors in the humid air of 28.3 %RH with different pre-bias voltages (V pre) ranging from -1 V to 2 V at 20 °C. (d) Improved transient I D behaviors in the humid air of 28.3 % RH at 20 °C. The pulsed pre-bias voltages (V pres) are 2 V and −1 V, respectively, in the response and recovery periods.

(a) Short-term repeatability of the FET-type humidity sensor in the humid air of 81.3 % RH at 20 °C. (b) Long-term stability of the humidity sensor to the humid air with relative humidity ranging from 28.3 % RH to 81.3 % RH. In all relative humidity levels, the response of the humidity sensor has no noticeable variation after more than 5 months.

head.ce:title: Highly stable Si MOSFET-type humidity sensor with ink-jet printed graphene quantum dots sensing layer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Seoul National University, Seoul, 08826, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering, Kookmin University, Seoul, 02707, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical Engineering, Kookmin University, Seoul 02707, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper investigates humidity sensing characteristics of a silicon metal oxide semiconductor field effect transistor (Si MOSFET)-based humidity sensor having horizontal floating-gate (FG) interdigitated with control-gate (CG). The sensing material of the humidity sensor is graphene quantum dots (GQDs), deposited locally on the interdigitated CG-FG area by an ink-jet printing method using a small amount of the GQDs solution. The humidity sensing characteristics of the sensor are measured as a parameter of relative humidity (RH). The response of the humidity sensor is 78 % to the humid air of 81.3 % RH. We also adopt a pulsed pre-bias method to improve the response and recovery characteristics of the humidity sensor. The response and recovery characteristics of the sensor can be improved 30 % and 40 % respectively by applying a pre-bias of 2 V and -1 V to the CG. In all relative humidity ranges, the FET-type humidity sensor has highly stable and reproducible characteristics in long-term measurements for 5 months.

ce:section.ce:para: Humidity is one of the essential environmental characteristics that influence the quality of the products in many industries and technologies. Therefore, humidity sensors for monitoring relative humidity are necessary in various fields such as semiconductor manufacturing and packaging, automotive, medical application, and environmental monitoring, and so on. In response to these demands, many studies have been conducted on the humidity sensors that meet the requirements of low cost, low power consumption, and high stability. So far, many sensing materials such as metal oxides, such as TiO2, ZnO, In2O3, polymer, and two dimensional (2D) materials, such as MoS2, WS2, have been developed for humidity sensors [1–7]. Among them, graphene-based materials (graphene, graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO)) have shown great potential for the detection of the humidity since they have high surface-to-volume ratio and special electrical characteristics [8–10]. Humidity sensors with graphene-based materials as a sensing layer have been developed using various sensing mechanisms, such as a resistive, impedance, capacitive, surface acoustic wave (SAW) and FET-type [11–15]. The resistor-type humidity sensors have the advantages of a simple process and high sensitivity, but they have demerits in size and current drift issues [16]. The thin film transistor (TFT) type humidity sensors are fabricated using simple process steps and have a high degree of freedom of operation due to its three terminals. But, they also have a large size and poor long-term stability [17]. A Si MOSFET-type transducer can be excellent candidates for humidity sensor since they have advantages of small size, low power consumption, and compatibility with complementary metal-oxide semiconductor (CMOS) process [18].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have physical and chemical properties such as a wide surface area and surface groups similar to those of graphene. Besides, physical and chemical properties of the GQDs can be changed depending on the size of the dots and the type or amount of doping [19,20]. Therefore, recently, GQDs have applied to various research fields, such as biomedicine, optoelectronic and sensing systems. [21–23]. Since graphene-based materials are sensitive to humidity, GQDs are also explored for sensing materials of the humidity sensors. The first humidity sensor with GQDs sensing layer was reported by Sreeprasad in 2013 [24]. In this paper, the humidity sensor detects the relative humidity by modulation in electronic tunneling distance across the hygroscopic polymer microfiber. However, the humidity sensor has a complicated fabrication process and the detectable humidity range is limited to 0–40 % RH. Except for this sensor, most GQDs-based humidity sensors adopted the resistor-type sensors [25–27]. The resistor-type humidity sensors are relatively large and use a large amount of GQDs solution.

In this work, a Si MOSFET with a horizontal floating gate (FG) interdigitated with a control gate (CG) is used as a transducer of the humidity sensor [28]. Although the Si MOSFET-type humidity sensors have a relatively lower sensitivity compared to that of other resistor-type humidity sensors with GQDs, the sensors are smaller in size and more reliable compared to the other humidity sensors. The GQDs sensing layer can be deposited on the CG-FG of the FET transducer using a small amount of the GQDs solution by ink-jet printing methods [25,27]. We investigate the humidity sensing performance of the fabricated humidity sensors with GQDs sensing layer when the relative humidity (RH) varies from 17.5 RH to 81.3 % RH. It also shows how the response and recovery characteristics of the humidity sensor are improved by the pulsed bias scheme. The FET-type humidity sensors are studied for how stable and reproducible properties they exhibit in short and long-term measurements.

The synthesis process of GQDs is performed as shown in supplementary Fig. 2a of the ref. 29 [29]. The GQDs were synthesized by chemical oxidation and cutting of carbon fibers(CFs) under strong acidic conditions. 0.9 g of CFs was added to 3:1 mixture of sulfuric acid (180 mL) and nitric acid (60 mL) and stirred for 1 day at 80 ℃. The solution was diluted and dialyzed with DI water for purification and filtered with 20 nm Anodisk filter. The final product was obtained by freeze drying in powder form. The 50 mg of synthesized GQDs powder are dispersed into the 50 mL of Dimethylformamide (HCON(CH3)2).

Fig. 1 a and b show the 3D schematic bird’s eye view and microscopic image of the FET-type humidity sensor with an ink-jet printed GQDs sensing layer. Fig. 1c shows the cross-sectional view of the humidity sensor cut along the line A-A’ in Fig. 1b. Key fabrication process of humidity sensor with the p-type FET transducer is depicted in Figure S1. The fabrication process starts with n-type six-inch single-crystalline silicon (Si) wafer. The silicon oxide (SiO2) and silicon nitride (Si3N4) layers are deposited sequentially and patterned to define active regions. Next, a 550 nm-thick field oxide is grown for isolation of the adjacent devices, which is referred to as the local oxidation of silicon (LOCOS) process. After the Si3N4 layer is removed and the SiO2 layer beneath it is also removed, a sacrificial SiO2 layer is grown. The boron ions are implanted to form a buried channel, leading to low flicker noise [30]. Then the sacrificial SiO2 layer is removed. After a 10 nm-thick gate oxide grown by dry oxidation, n +-doped polysilicon is deposited to form a FG. Boron ions are implanted to form p + source/drain regions. To prevent external contamination, a SiO2 (10 nm)/ Si3N4 (20 nm)/ SiO2 (10 nm) (O/N/O) stack is formed for passivation layer. After defining contact holes, Cr (30 nm)/ Au (50 nm) metal stack for CG is formed. Before ink-jet printing of the sensing layer, a SU-8 photoresist is patterned to open the interdigitated FG-CG area, represented as a white dashed line in Fig. 1b.

In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are used as a sensing layer of the humidity sensor. The GQDs sensing layer is deposited by using the ink-jet printer (Omni Jet 100, Unijet) with a cartridge (Fujifilm) filled with the GQDs solution (1 mg/mL). The GQDs sensing layer can be formed on FET-type sensors using a much smaller amount of GQDs solution (∼ hundreds pL) compared to that in other resistor-type sensors with the GQDs sensing layer [25,27]. After ink-jet printing, the annealing of the humidity sensor is performed at 200 °C for 10 min to remove the solvent of the GQDs solution.

In this work, humidity sensing characteristics of the FET-type sensor are measured using the humidity sensing measurement system depicted in Figure S2. The humid air is produced by passing the dry air (3.4 % RH) through the humidifier. Mass flow controller (MFC) controls the gas flow rates of the dry air used as reference gas and the dry air passing through humidifier, respectively. The humidity of the gas injected into the mixing chamber is adjusted by controlling the ratio of the dry air and humid air. The relative humidity of the mixing gas is measured by a commercial humidity sensor before injecting the gas into the test chamber. In the test chamber, the sensing characteristics of the FET-type humidity sensor is measured using a probe station (MSTECH) and a semiconductor parameter analyzer (B1500A, Agilent Technologies) with a waveform generator and fast measurement unit (WGFMU) module.

Fig. 2 a and b show that a transmission electron microscope (TEM) image and particle size distribution of the synthesized GQDs. The GQDs are formed in a relatively uniform size, about 2.01 ± 0.33 nm (Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 2c, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the GQDs has a dominant signal at 284 eV, which is assigned to the sp2 carbons (CC groups). Whereas the signals at 285.1 eV and 288.6 eV corresponding to carbonyl (CO groups) and hydroxyl (CO groups) contribute to 19.37 % and 18.25 % respectively. Next, Fig. 2d shows that Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of the GQDs in the range of 1000 ∼ 4000 cm−1. The FT-IR spectra were obtained by using a JASCO FT/IR-4600 spectrometer. The FT-IR spectra of the GQDs have strong and broadened peaks at 3422 cm−1 and also strong peaks at 1629 cm−1 and 1719 cm−1. The results of the FT-IR spectra also show that the GQDs contain the sp2 carbons and the functional groups (OH, COOH).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Humidity sensing performance of the FET-type sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a shows the transfer characteristics (I D – V CG) of the FET-type humidity sensor as a parameter of the relative humidity from 3.4 % RH to 81.3 % RH at 20 °C. Both channel width (W) and length (L) of the humidity sensor are 2 μm. The transfer characteristics are measured at a fixed drain-source voltage (V DS) of -0.1 V. As the relative humidity increases, the transfer curve gradually shifts to the left. Fig. 3b shows schematically the adsorption of the water molecules on the surface of the GQDs sensing layer. The adsorption of the water molecules on the surface of the GQDs sensing layer depends on the relative humidity level. At low relative humidity, water molecules are chemisorbed on the GQDs sensing layer [16,25]. Water molecules behave depending on the surrounding environment since water is a poor oxidizing and reducing agent [31]. In this work, water molecules act as an electron donor, donating electrons to the GQDs sensing layer. This leads to increase the resistance of the GQDs, which is known as a p-type material [31–34]. Water molecules donate electrons to GQDs during adsorption and are positively ionized. In particular, positive water molecules adsorbed at the interface between ONO covering the FG and GQDs induce negative charges in the channel of the FET [35]. Therefore, the humidity response of the sensor causes the threshold voltage (V th) of the humidity sensor to increase in a negative direction (Fig. 3a). Then, the drain current (I D) of the pFET-type humidity sensor decreases as the relative humidity increases as shown in Fig. 4 a.

As the relative humidity increases, water molecules are physisorbed on the chemisorbed water molecules through hydrogen bonds [16]. When the relative humidity further increases, multilayer water molecules are adsorbed as shown in Fig. 3b. These multilayer water molecules gradually exhibit the liquid-like behaviors. In the liquid multilayer, the physisorbed water molecules are ionized into the H3O+ ions as charge carriers under the electrostatic fields. The proton hopping occurs between adjacent water molecules via ionic conductivity generated by the Grotthus chain reaction (Fig. 3b) [16]. In this work, these physisorbed water molecules can induce dipoles at the surface of the GQDs sensing layer. The induced dipoles cause the work-function of the sensing layer to shift upwards uniformly by a certain amount [36,37]. Therefore, at high relative humidity, the V th of the humidity sensor is shifted to the negative bias direction, and then I D decreases. Fig. 4b shows the response of the humidity sensor derived from Fig. 4a. The response (S) of the humidity sensor is defined as
(1)
S ( % ) =
I
D , h u m i d
−
I
D , d r y
I
D , d r y
× 100
Here, I D,dry is the drain current at dry air (3.4 % RH) ambience, and I D,humid is the drain current when saturated after injecting of the humid air. The response of the humidity sensor increases and gradually saturation as the relative humidity increases from 3.4 % RH to 81.3 % RH (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c shows the |I D| curve of the humidity sensor as a function of relative humidity. The sensitivity of the humidity sensor is 7 nA/%RH regarding the whole relative humidity range (3.4–81.3 % RH). However, the response of the humidity sensor gradually saturation as the relative humidity increases. Therefore, the humidity sensor has a higher sensitivity (10 nA/%RH) when only considered in the lower humidity range (3.4–47.2 % RH) as shown in the inset of Fig. 4c.

Fig. 5 a shows transient I D behaviors of the humidity sensor as a parameter of the operating temperature in the humid air of 81.3 %RH. The humidity sensor has the best sensing performance at 20 °C (Fig. 5a). As the operating temperature increases, the response of the humidity sensor in humid air of 81.3 % RH greatly decreases from 72.3%–12.8%. Fig. 5b shows the response of the humidity sensor for different relative humidity levels (28.3–81.3 % RH) measured at different operating temperatures (20−60 °C). Fig. 5c shows the response and response time of the humidity sensor as a function of operating temperature. The response time is defined as the time required to reach 90 % of the saturation response after NO2 gas injection. The response and response time decreases as the operating temperature increases from 20 °C to 60 °C (Fig. 5c).

In our previous work, a pulsed pre-bias scheme for improving the sensing characteristics of the gas sensor has been reported [35,38]. Fig. 6 a depicts the schematic diagram of the pulsed pre-bias scheme used in this work. The WGFMU modules generate the pulse scheme consisting of a pre-bias voltage (V pre) and a read bias voltage (V rCG). The pre-bias voltage in the range of -1 to 2 V is applied to the CG of the humidity sensor during the pre-bias time (tpre). At the same time, a drain bias (V DS) of -0.1 V is applied to the drain of the humidity sensor in synchronization with the V rCG. Fig. 6b shows the response of the humidity sensor depending on the pre-bias voltage applied to the CG. The response of the humidity sensor increases as positive pre-bias applied to the CG increases, but the recovery time is rather longer. When the positive pre-bias is applied to CG, water molecules easily release electrons and are adsorbed at the interface between the GQDs sensing layer and O/N/O passivation layer since there is a lack of electrons at the interface compared to the other part. This leads to induce the negative charge in the channel. Therefore, the V th of the humidity sensor increases more in the humid air condition. On the other hand, the response and recovery time of the humidity sensor decreases when the negative pre-bias is applied to CG as shown in Fig. 6b. The negative pre-bias easily gives electrons to positively ionized water molecules at the interface between GQDs sensing layer and ONO passivation layer. This allows water molecules to be desorbed from the sensing material, allowing the sensing layer to be easily recovered in the dry air condition. The drain current of the humidity sensor returns to its original level faster when the negative pre-bias is applied to the CG (Fig. 6b). The positive pre-bias increases the humidity response of the sensor and the negative pre-bias increases the recovery speed of the sensor. Therefore, the response and recovery characteristics of the humidity sensor can be maximized by applying the positive and negative pre-bias to the CG in the response and recovery periods, respectively (Fig. 6c).

We investigate the reproducibility and long-term stability of the FET-type humidity sensor with the GQDs sensing layer. The reproducibility of the humidity sensor is measured by injecting the dry and humid air (81.3 % RH) repeatedly into the test chamber. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the humidity sensing characteristics of the sensor remain unchanged even after the repeated short-term operation. The long-term stability of the humidity sensor is measured by injecting humid air with different relative humidity (28.3, 47.2, 62.1, and 81.3 %RH) per month for 5 months (Fig. 7b). Please note that the humidity sensor has been exposed to the air for 5 months except when measuring. In all range of the relative humidity, the response of the humidity sensor almost constant response characteristics after more than 5 months.

We have investigated the humidity sensing characteristics of the FET-type humidity sensor with GQDs as a sensing layer. In this work, the p-type FET was used as a transducer of the humidity sensor because of the low flicker noise. The GQDs sensing layer was deposited by ink-jet printing method using a smaller amount of the GQD solution (∼ hundreds pL), compared to that of other reported resistor-type humidity sensors. The humidity sensing characteristics were measured when the relative humidity varies from 3.4 % RH to 81.3 % RH. As relative humidity increases, a response of the humidity sensor increases, but the slope of the increase decreases. The FET-type humidity sensor has best performance at 20 °C. As the operating temperature increases, the response of the humidity sensor decreases. When 2 V of positive pre-bias was applied to the CG, the response of the sensor was increased by 30 %. Otherwise, the recovery characteristics of the sensor was improved when −1 V of negative pre-bias was applied to the CG. Therefore, the response and recovery time could be maximized by applying the positive and negative pre-bias in the response and recovery periods respectively. The humidity sensor also showed stable repeatability, and showed almost constant response characteristics even when measured for 5 months. The fabricated FET-type humidity sensor is expected to be a promising humidity sensor that has highly sensitive and stable humidity sensing characteristics.

Yujeong Jeong: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Methodology, Validation. Seongbin Hong: Methodology. Gyuweon Jung: Validation. Wonjun Shin: Validation. Jinwoo Park: Formal analysis. Donghee Kim: Formal analysis. Yong Seok Choi: Methodology. Jong-Ho Bae: Formal analysis. Byung Hee Hong: Formal analysis. Jong-Ho Lee: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Brain Korea 21 Plus Project in 2021 and the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF-2016R1A2B3009361).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130134.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Proceedings of IEEE International Electron Devices Meeting (IEDM)

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yujeong Jeong received the B.S. degree in Electrical and Computer Engineering from Seoul National University (SNU), Seoul, Korea in 2017. She is currently working toward a combined master’s and doctorate program in Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at SNU. She is also with the Inter-University Semiconductor Research Center, SNU. Her current research interests include Si MOSFET-based sensor platform design and sensing materials.

Seongbin Hong received the B.S. degree in Electrical and Computer Engineering from Seoul National University (SNU), Seoul, Korea in 2016. He is currently working toward a combined master’s and doctorate program in Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at SNU. He is also with the Inter-University Semiconductor Research Center, SNU. His current research interests include Si MOSFET-based sensor platform design and fabrication.

Gyuweon Jung received the B.S. degree in Electrical and Computer Engineering from Seoul National University (SNU), Seoul, Korea in 2018. He is currently working toward a combined master’s and doctorate program in Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at SNU. He is also with the Inter-University Semiconductor Research Center, SNU. His current research interests include Si MOSFET-based sensor platform design and gas identification.

Wonjun Shin received the B.S. degree in Electrical and Computer Engineering from Seoul National University (SNU), Seoul, Korea in 2017. He is currently working toward a combined master’s and doctorate program in Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at SNU. He is also with the Inter-University Semiconductor Research Center, SNU. His current research interests include noise analysis of Si MOSFET-based sensor.

Jinwoo Park received the B.S. degree in Electrical and Computer Engineering from Seoul National University (SNU), Seoul, Korea in 2018. He is currently working toward a combined master’s and doctorate program in Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at SNU. He is also with the Inter-University Semiconductor Research Center, SNU. His current research interests include Si MOSFET-based sensor measurement.

Donghee Kim received the B.S. degree in Electrical Engineering from Korea University (KU) in 2020. He is currently working toward a combined master’s and doctorate program in Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at SNU. He is also with the Inter-University Semiconductor Research Center, SNU. His current research interests include Si MOSFET-based sensor measurement.

Yong Seok Choi received his Ph.D. degree in Chemistry from Seoul National University in 2019. Then he joined Graphene Square Inc. as deputy director. His current research interests are focused on the mass production and applications of 2D nanomaterials.

Jong-Ho Bae received the B.S. degree in Electrical and Electronic Engineering from Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH), Pohang, Korea in 2011, and the Ph.D. degree in Electrical and Computer Engineering from Seoul National University (SNU), Seoul, Korea in 2018. He worked as a postdoctoral researcher at the department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences, University of California at Berkeley. Since September 2020, he has been a Professor in the School of Electrical Engineering, Kookmin University. His current research interests include CMOS technology, nonvolatile memory device, gas sensor and neuromorphic system and devices.

Byung Hee Hong received BS (1998), MS (2000) and PhD (2002) degrees in chemistry from the Pohang University of Science and Technology. After spending 3.5 years as a post-doc at Columbia University (Advisor: Philip Kim), he joined the Department of Chemistry at Sungkyunkwan University as an assistant professor in 2007. Now he is an associate professor in the Department of Chemistry at Seoul National University

Jong-Ho Lee received the B.S. degree from Kyungpook National University (KNU), Daegu, Korea, in 1987 and the M.S. and Ph.D. degrees from Seoul National University (SNU), Seoul, in 1989 and 1993, respectively, all in electronic engineering. In 1993, he worked on advanced BiCMOS process development at ISRC, SNU as an engineer. In 1994, he was with the School of Electrical Engineering, Wonkwang University, Iksan, Chonpuk, Korea. In 2002, he moved to KNU, as a Professor of the School of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science. Since September 2009, he has been a Professor in the School of Electrical and Computer Engineering, SNU. He is a Lifetime Member of the Institute of Electronics Engineers of Korea (IEEK) and IEEE Fellow. He has been served as a subcommittee member of IEDM, ITRS ERD member, a general chair of IPFA2011, and IEEE EDS Korea chapter chair. He received many awards for excellent research papers and research excellence. He invented bulk FinFET, Saddle FinFET (or bCAT) for DRAM cell transistors, and NAND flash cell string with virtual source/drain. These three inventions are key technologies, which have been applying for mass production in industry.
